title: Getting your conjoint data into shape
text: conjointmatchups expects data profile-long form: one row per candidate profile per choice task, two profiles per task. row needs columns key profile: respondent id, task id (unique within respondent), profile index (two candidates task row ); binary chosen indicator: 1 profile picked task, 0 otherwise, exactly one chosen per task; one column per randomized attribute (party, gender, ). names . Every function takes key column names arguments. shipped kc_yougov dataset (YouGov sample Kirkland Coppock 2018) exactly form: respondent, task, profile key row; chosen outcome; party experience randomized attributes; resp_party respondent-level covariate carried along subgroup analysis. Attributes may NA feature shown given condition. data party shown partisan condition (substantive point study), NA rest. fine: matchups party simply use tasks party shown.
code: head(kc_yougov, 6) #>   respondent task profile chosen       party gender     race     age #> 1          1    1       1      0        <NA>    man Hispanic   older #> 2          1    1       2      1        <NA>    man    Black   older #> 3          1    2       1      1 Independent    man    Black younger #> 4          1    2       2      0 Independent  woman Hispanic   older #> 5          1    3       1      1        <NA>    man Hispanic younger #> 6          1    3       2      0        <NA>    man Hispanic younger #>      occupation       experience resp_party #> 1  professional prior experience   Democrat #> 2       teacher prior experience   Democrat #> 3      business prior experience   Democrat #> 4      business prior experience   Democrat #> 5          <NA> prior experience   Democrat #> 6 working class prior experience   Democrat

what: If your data is already task-wide
title: Getting your conjoint data into shape
text: instead one row per task per-profile columns (party_1, party_2, chosen_1, chosen_2, …), use as_profiles() get profile-long, skip straight get_matchups(), operates task-wide data directly (see ).

title: Getting your conjoint data into shape
text: anything else, make sure can point : raw export candidate attributes spread across oddly named columns, reshape table first (tidyr), continue.

what: Step 1: reshape to task-wide with as_tasks()
title: Getting your conjoint data into shape
text: Matchup extraction compares two profiles within task, works task-wide representation: one row per task, every attribute suffixed profile index. as_profiles() exact inverse ever need go back.
code: tasks <- as_tasks(   kc_yougov,   task_keys = c("respondent", "task"),   profile = "profile",   outcome = "chosen" ) tasks[, c("respondent", "task", "party_1", "party_2", "chosen_1", "chosen_2")] |>   head(4) #> # A tibble: 4 × 6 #>   respondent  task party_1     party_2     chosen_1 chosen_2 #>        <int> <int> <chr>       <chr>          <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1          1     1 NA          NA                 0        1 #> 2          1     2 Independent Independent        1        0 #> 3          1     3 NA          NA                 1        0 #> 4          1     4 Democrat    Independent        1        0

what: Step 2: extract a matchup with get_matchups()
title: Getting your conjoint data into shape
text: matchup subset tasks one profile carries attributes pin carries B, either display position. result renames outcome clean binary, A_wins. can pin several attributes hold comparison fixed, example younger woman versus older man:
code: dem_v_rep <- get_matchups(   tasks,   A = list(party = "Democrat"),   B = list(party = "Republican"),   outcome = "chosen" ) head(dem_v_rep, 4) #> # A tibble: 4 × 3 #>   respondent  task A_wins #>        <int> <int>  <int> #> 1          3     4      0 #> 2          6     4      0 #> 3          8     1      0 #> 4          9     4      0 mean(dem_v_rep$A_wins)  # share of Democrat wins in these head-to-heads #> [1] 0.5893471 young_woman_v_old_man <- get_matchups(   tasks,   A = list(gender = "woman", age = "younger"),   B = list(gender = "man",   age = "older"),   outcome = "chosen" ) mean(young_woman_v_old_man$A_wins) #> [1] 0.5802708

previous_headings: Step 2: extract a matchup with get_matchups()
title: Getting your conjoint data into shape
text: B must mutually exclusive: must differ least one shared attribute. Otherwise single profile satisfy sides “estimate” reflect candidate shown first, real difference. get_matchups() refuses contrast, can check :
code: valid_contrast(list(party = "Democrat"), list(party = "Republican")) #> [1] TRUE valid_contrast(list(party = "Democrat"), list(gender = "woman")) #> [1] FALSE

title: Getting your conjoint data into shape
text: matchup ready estimator. afcp() thin convenience computes Average Feature Choice Probability, chance -side wins, respondent-clustered standard errors: richer estimands (AMCE, marginal means) hand matchup, task-wide data, cregg cjoint. conjointmatchups stops producing analysis-ready dataset.
code: afcp(dem_v_rep, clusters = "respondent") #> # A tibble: 1 × 5 #>   estimate std.error conf.low conf.high     n #>      <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> <int> #> 1    0.589    0.0227    0.545     0.634   582

code: @Manual{,   title = {conjointmatchups: Reshape and Extract Matchups from Forced-Choice Conjoint Experiments},   author = {Alexander Coppock},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.0.0.9000},   url = {https://github.com/acoppock/conjointmatchups}, }

what: Reshape and Extract Matchups from Forced-Choice Conjoint Experiments
title: Reshape and Extract Matchups from Forced-Choice Conjoint Experiments
text: conjointmatchups provides data grammar forced-choice (paired) conjoint experiments: lossless reshaping two natural representations data, extraction matchups, analysis-ready pairwise sub-experiments let ask “candidate features faced candidate features, won?” intentionally small. estimate AMCEs marginal means. produces datasets estimators consume.

title: Reshape and Extract Matchups from Forced-Choice Conjoint Experiments
text: Every forced-choice conjoint task two things : Profile-long form (one row per candidate profile): natural describing randomizing attributes. Task-wide form (one row per choice task, per-profile columns like party_1, party_2): natural modeling binary choice. Analysts hand-roll pivot constantly, get wrong: drop pairing, misalign outcome, silently reorder profiles. conjointmatchups makes pivot canonical lossless (as_tasks(), as_profiles()), adds move single-experiment packages skip: restricting tasks realize controlled contrast two profiles renaming outcome clean binary (get_matchups()). comes ready lm, glm, estimatr::lm_robust(), random-effects meta-analysis across many studies.

what: The one thing to get right: your input format
title: Reshape and Extract Matchups from Forced-Choice Conjoint Experiments
text: conjointmatchups expects profile-long data: one row per candidate profile per choice task, two profiles per task. row needs columns key profile (respondent id, task id, profile index), binary chosen indicator (exactly one profile chosen per task), one column per randomized attribute. names choose; every function takes key column names arguments. shipped kc_yougov dataset (YouGov sample Kirkland Coppock 2018) exactly shape: Attributes may NA feature shown condition (party appears partisan arm). raw export shaped differently, reshape table first. already task-wide (party_1, party_2, …), as_profiles() gets , hand straight get_matchups(). Get started vignette walks whole path.
code: library(conjointmatchups) head(kc_yougov) #>   respondent  task profile chosen party       gender race     age    occupation   experience       resp_party #>            1     1       1      0 NA          man    Hispanic older  professional prior experience Democrat #>            1     1       2      1 NA          man    Black    older  teacher      prior experience Democrat #>            1     2       1      1 Independent man    Black    younger business    prior experience Democrat #>            1     2       2      0 Independent woman  Hispanic older  business     prior experience Democrat

title: Reshape and Extract Matchups from Forced-Choice Conjoint Experiments
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("acoppock/conjointmatchups")

title: Reshape and Extract Matchups from Forced-Choice Conjoint Experiments
text: get_matchups() refuses ill-defined contrast (one single profile satisfy sides), “comparison” measures display position, real difference. valid_contrast() contrast_message() expose check building interfaces.
code: library(conjointmatchups)  # profile-long -> task-wide tasks <- as_tasks(profiles, task_keys = c("study_id", "resp_id", "task_id"),                   profile = "profile", outcome = "chosen")  # extract a Republican-vs-Democrat matchup, outcome renamed to A_wins m <- get_matchups(tasks,                   A = list(party = "Republican"),                   B = list(party = "Democrat"),                   outcome = "chosen")  # estimate the AFCP, respondent-clustered, per study afcp(m, by = "study_id", clusters = "resp_id", weights = "resp_weights")

what: Where it sits in the conjoint R ecosystem
title: Reshape and Extract Matchups from Forced-Choice Conjoint Experiments
text: existing conjoint packages analyze single experiment jump straight estimand. None owns reshape--select layer beneath step, exactly conjointmatchups provides. relationship AFCP estimand deliberate. conjointmatchups produces matchup datasets AFCP defined : pair feature levels, restrict tasks pitting compute probability one wins (Abramson, Kocak, Magazinnik, Strezhnev). included afcp() thin convenience estimatr::lm_robust() closes loop; estimator’s inferential machinery preference-cycle tests, use afcp package directly, AMCE/MM analyses single experiment reach cregg cjoint.

title: Reshape and Extract Matchups from Forced-Choice Conjoint Experiments
text: Column names . Every function takes key column names (task keys, profile index, outcome stem) arguments; nothing hard-coded particular coding scheme. Dependency-light core. reshape matchup functions depend dplyr, tidyr, tibble, application (e.g. Shiny explorer) can depend data grammar without pulling estimation stack. estimatr required afcp().

title: Reshape and Extract Matchups from Forced-Choice Conjoint Experiments
text: Abramson, S. F., Kocak, K., Magazinnik, ., Strezhnev, . Detecting preference cycles forced-choice conjoint experiments. Hainmueller, J., Hopkins, D. J., Yamamoto, T. (2014). Causal inference conjoint analysis: Understanding multidimensional choices via stated preference experiments. Political Analysis, 22(1), 1–30. Leeper, T. J., Hobolt, S. B., Tilley, J. (2020). Measuring subgroup preferences conjoint experiments. Political Analysis, 28(2), 207–221.

what: Average Feature Choice Probability from a matchup — afcp
title: Average Feature Choice Probability from a matchup — afcp
text: convenience wrapper closes loop matchup workflow: given output get_matchups(), estimates probability -side profile chosen (AFCP), optionally within respondent-clustered, weighted groups. estimation one-liner estimatr::lm_robust(); function exists make as_profiles() |> as_tasks() |> get_matchups() |> afcp() complete workflow. richer estimands (AMCE, marginal means) use 'cregg' 'cjoint'.

title: Average Feature Choice Probability from a matchup — afcp
code: afcp(   matchups,   outcome = "A_wins",   by = NULL,   clusters = NULL,   weights = NULL,   se_type = "stata" )

title: Average Feature Choice Probability from a matchup — afcp
text: matchups data frame get_matchups(). outcome Name binary outcome column. Defaults "A_wins". Optional character vector grouping columns (e.g. "study_id"); one AFCP returned per group. clusters Optional name cluster column (e.g. "resp_id") cluster-robust standard errors. weights Optional name survey-weight column. se_type Standard-error type passed estimatr::lm_robust().

title: Average Feature Choice Probability from a matchup — afcp
text: tibble tidy estimates: grouping columns () plus estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high, count observations. estimate AFCP.

title: Average Feature Choice Probability from a matchup — afcp
code: # \donttest{ tasks <- data.frame(   study_id = "s1", resp_id = rep(1:20, each = 2),   party_1  = rep(c("R", "D"), 20), party_2 = rep(c("D", "R"), 20),   chosen_1 = rep(c(1, 0), 20),      chosen_2 = rep(c(0, 1), 20) ) m <- get_matchups(tasks, list(party = "R"), list(party = "D"),                   outcome = "chosen") if (requireNamespace("estimatr", quietly = TRUE)) {   afcp(m, by = "study_id", clusters = "resp_id") } #> # A tibble: 1 × 6 #>   study_id estimate std.error conf.low conf.high     n #>   <chr>       <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> <int> #> 1 s1              1         0        1         1    40 # }

what: Reshape task-level (wide) conjoint data to profile-level (long) — as_profiles
title: Reshape task-level (wide) conjoint data to profile-level (long) — as_profiles
text: inverse as_tasks(). Columns matching x{sep}{profile value} pivoted back one row per profile per task; columns match (task-level respondent-level covariates) recycled across profiles.

title: Reshape task-level (wide) conjoint data to profile-level (long) — as_profiles
code: as_profiles(   tasks,   task_keys,   profiles = c("1", "2"),   sep = "_",   profile_to = "profile" )

title: Reshape task-level (wide) conjoint data to profile-level (long) — as_profiles
text: tasks data frame task-wide form. task_keys Character vector column names identifying task. Together profile_to key output. profiles Character vector profile values unstack. Defaults c("1", "2"). sep Separator attribute name profile suffix. profile_to Name new profile-index column output.

title: Reshape task-level (wide) conjoint data to profile-level (long) — as_profiles
text: tibble profile-long form.

title: Reshape task-level (wide) conjoint data to profile-level (long) — as_profiles
code: tasks <- data.frame(   task_id  = c(1, 2),   party_1  = c("R", "D"), party_2 = c("D", "R"),   chosen_1 = c(1, 0),      chosen_2 = c(0, 1) ) as_profiles(tasks, task_keys = "task_id") #> # A tibble: 4 × 4 #>   task_id profile party chosen #>     <dbl> <chr>   <chr> <chr>  #> 1       1 1       R     1      #> 2       1 2       D     0      #> 3       2 1       D     0      #> 4       2 2       R     1

what: Reshape profile-level (long) conjoint data to task-level (wide) — as_tasks
title: Reshape profile-level (long) conjoint data to task-level (wide) — as_tasks
text: choice task appears two profile rows input. as_tasks() pivots rows single wide row per task, suffixing every non-key column profile index (e.g. party becomes party_1 party_2). representation used modeling binary choice get_matchups().

title: Reshape profile-level (long) conjoint data to task-level (wide) — as_tasks
code: as_tasks(profiles, task_keys, profile, outcome = NULL, sep = "_")

title: Reshape profile-level (long) conjoint data to task-level (wide) — as_tasks
text: profiles data frame profile-long form: one row per candidate profile per task. task_keys Character vector column names jointly identify choice task (e.g. c("study_id", "resp_id", "task_id")). carried unchanged. profile Name column indexing profile within task (values 1/2). values become column-name suffixes. outcome Optional name choice-indicator column. supplied, resulting per-profile outcome columns coerced numeric (character pivoting, like every attribute). sep Separator attribute name profile suffix. Defaults "_".

title: Reshape profile-level (long) conjoint data to task-level (wide) — as_tasks
text: tibble one row per task: task_keys plus, every input column x every profile value p, column x{sep}p.

title: Reshape profile-level (long) conjoint data to task-level (wide) — as_tasks
code: profiles <- data.frame(   task_id = c(1, 1, 2, 2),   profile = c(1, 2, 1, 2),   party   = c("R", "D", "D", "R"),   chosen  = c(1, 0, 0, 1) ) as_tasks(profiles, task_keys = "task_id", profile = "profile",          outcome = "chosen") #> # A tibble: 2 × 5 #>   task_id party_1 chosen_1 party_2 chosen_2 #>     <dbl> <chr>      <dbl> <chr>      <dbl> #> 1       1 R              1 D              0 #> 2       2 D              0 R              1

what: Check that task-wide data is a valid forced-choice conjoint — check_conjoint
title: Check that task-wide data is a valid forced-choice conjoint — check_conjoint
text: Verifies invariants binary forced-choice conjoint must satisfy task-wide form: two per-profile outcome columns exist, values 0/1, exactly one profile chosen every task. Generalizes structural checks used cleaning conjoint study.

title: Check that task-wide data is a valid forced-choice conjoint — check_conjoint
code: check_conjoint(tasks, outcome = "chosen", profiles = c("1", "2"), sep = "_")

title: Check that task-wide data is a valid forced-choice conjoint — check_conjoint
text: tasks data frame task-wide form (see as_tasks()). outcome Name (stem) per-profile choice-indicator columns. profiles Length-2 character vector profile suffixes. sep Separator attribute stem profile suffix.

title: Check that task-wide data is a valid forced-choice conjoint — check_conjoint
text: Invisibly, tasks. Called side effect erroring violated invariant.

title: Check that task-wide data is a valid forced-choice conjoint — check_conjoint
code: tasks <- data.frame(chosen_1 = c(1, 0), chosen_2 = c(0, 1)) check_conjoint(tasks)

what: conjointmatchups: the data grammar of forced-choice conjoint experiments — conjointmatchups-package
title: conjointmatchups: the data grammar of forced-choice conjoint experiments — conjointmatchups-package
text: Forced-choice (paired) conjoint data fundamental duality. choice task two profile rows (long form, natural describing attributes) one task row per-profile columns (wide form, natural modeling binary choice). conjointmatchups provides lossless pivot two representations (as_tasks(), as_profiles()) way extract matchups (get_matchups()): subset tasks realizing specified contrast two profiles, returned clean binary outcome ready hand estimator.

title: conjointmatchups: the data grammar of forced-choice conjoint experiments — conjointmatchups-package
text: package deliberately re-implement AMCE marginal-means estimation (see 'cregg', 'cjoint', 'projoint', 'factorEx'). produces analysis-ready datasets tools consume, underlies Average Feature Choice Probability (AFCP) estimand. thin afcp() convenience closes loop AFCP workflow.

title: conjointmatchups: the data grammar of forced-choice conjoint experiments — conjointmatchups-package
text: Maintainer: Alexander Coppock acoppock@gmail.com Authors: Alexander Coppock acoppock@gmail.com contributors: Matthew Blyth [contributor]

what: Explain why a matchup contrast is not well defined — contrast_message
title: Explain why a matchup contrast is not well defined — contrast_message
text: Produces human-readable message contrast rejected valid_contrast(), use error messages interactive UIs.

title: Explain why a matchup contrast is not well defined — contrast_message
code: contrast_message(A, B)

title: Explain why a matchup contrast is not well defined — contrast_message
text: , B Named lists mapping attribute stems pinned levels.

title: Explain why a matchup contrast is not well defined — contrast_message
text: length-1 character string.

title: Explain why a matchup contrast is not well defined — contrast_message
code: contrast_message(list(party = "R"), list(party = "R")) #> [1] "Candidates A and B are identical on: party. A clean comparison needs them to differ on a shared attribute, e.g. A: party = Republican vs B: party = Democrat. As specified, the matchup compares identical profiles and reflects which position was shown first, not a real difference."

what: Extract matchups realizing a contrast between two profiles — get_matchups
title: Extract matchups realizing a contrast between two profiles — get_matchups
text: matchup subset choice tasks one profile carries attributes pinned carries attributes pinned B (either display position). get_matchups() filters tasks rows returns single binary outcome, winner, equal 1 -side profile chosen. turns arbitrary conjoint clean pairwise sub-experiment ready estimation (see afcp()).

title: Extract matchups realizing a contrast between two profiles — get_matchups
code: get_matchups(   tasks,   A,   B,   outcome,   profiles = c("1", "2"),   sep = "_",   winner = "A_wins",   keep = NULL,   check_contrast = TRUE )

title: Extract matchups realizing a contrast between two profiles — get_matchups
text: tasks data frame task-wide form (see as_tasks()). , B Named lists mapping attribute stems pinned levels side. outcome Name (stem) per-profile choice-indicator columns (e.g. "chosen" columns chosen_1, chosen_2). Values must 0/1. profiles Length-2 character vector profile suffixes. Defaults c("1", "2"). sep Separator attribute stem profile suffix. winner Name returned binary outcome column. Defaults "A_wins". keep Character vector columns carry result. NULL (default), every column profile-specific (.e. end sep + profile suffix) kept: task keys, respondent covariates, weights, . check_contrast TRUE, error contrast mutually exclusive.

title: Extract matchups realizing a contrast between two profiles — get_matchups
text: tibble matched tasks: keep columns plus winner column (0/1).

title: Extract matchups realizing a contrast between two profiles — get_matchups
text: B named lists mapping attribute names (stems, without profile suffix) level side pinned . empty list leaves side unconstrained. contrast well defined, B must mutually exclusive: must pin least one shared attribute different levels. Otherwise single profile can satisfy sides "estimate" reflects display position (primacy), real difference. check_contrast = TRUE (default) invalid contrast raises error explaining ; see valid_contrast().

title: Extract matchups realizing a contrast between two profiles — get_matchups
code: tasks <- data.frame(   study_id = "s1", resp_id = c(1, 1, 2, 2),   party_1  = c("R", "D", "R", "R"), party_2 = c("D", "R", "R", "D"),   chosen_1 = c(1, 0, 1, 1),          chosen_2 = c(0, 1, 0, 0) ) get_matchups(tasks, A = list(party = "R"), B = list(party = "D"),              outcome = "chosen") #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   study_id resp_id A_wins #>   <chr>      <dbl>  <int> #> 1 s1             1      1 #> 2 s1             1      1 #> 3 s1             2      1

what: Kirkland & Coppock (2018) YouGov candidate conjoint — kc_yougov
title: Kirkland & Coppock (2018) YouGov candidate conjoint — kc_yougov
text: YouGov sample (Study 2) Kirkland Coppock's study candidate choice, profile-long form package expects input: one row per candidate profile per choice task, two profiles per task. Respondents made repeated binary forced choices pairs hypothetical candidates described randomized attributes.

title: Kirkland & Coppock (2018) YouGov candidate conjoint — kc_yougov
code: kc_yougov

title: Kirkland & Coppock (2018) YouGov candidate conjoint — kc_yougov
text: tibble 11,432 rows (5,716 tasks x 2 profiles) 11 columns: respondent Integer respondent id. task Integer task number within respondent (1-5). profile Profile index within task, 1 2. chosen 1 profile chosen task, else 0. Exactly one profile chosen per task. party Candidate party: "Democrat", "Republican", "Independent", NA nonpartisan condition. gender Candidate gender: "man" "woman". race Candidate race: "White", "Black", "Hispanic", "Asian". age Candidate age: "younger" "older". occupation Candidate occupation: "business", "professional", "teacher", "working class", NA shown. experience Political experience: "prior experience" "experience". resp_party Respondent's party identification (respondent-level covariate, constant within respondent): "Democrat", "Republican", "Independent". Useful subgroup analyses.

title: Kirkland & Coppock (2018) YouGov candidate conjoint — kc_yougov
text: Kirkland, P. ., & Coppock, . (2018). Candidate Choice without Party Labels: New Insights Conjoint Survey Experiments. Political Behavior, 40(3), 571-591. doi:10.1007/s11109-017-9414-8

title: Kirkland & Coppock (2018) YouGov candidate conjoint — kc_yougov
text: shape data calling as_tasks(): set columns key profile (respondent, task, profile index), binary chosen indicator, one column per randomized attribute. Attribute columns may NA feature shown given condition (party occupation shown respondents, substantive point study).

title: Kirkland & Coppock (2018) YouGov candidate conjoint — kc_yougov
code: head(kc_yougov) #> # A tibble: 6 × 11 #>   respondent  task profile chosen party gender race  age   occupation experience #>        <int> <int>   <int>  <int> <chr> <chr>  <chr> <chr> <chr>      <chr>      #> 1          1     1       1      0 NA    man    Hisp… older professio… prior exp… #> 2          1     1       2      1 NA    man    Black older teacher    prior exp… #> 3          1     2       1      1 Inde… man    Black youn… business   prior exp… #> 4          1     2       2      0 Inde… woman  Hisp… older business   prior exp… #> 5          1     3       1      1 NA    man    Hisp… youn… NA         prior exp… #> 6          1     3       2      0 NA    man    Hisp… youn… working c… prior exp… #> # ℹ 1 more variable: resp_party <chr> # reshape to task-wide, then extract a partisan matchup tasks <- as_tasks(kc_yougov,                   task_keys = c("respondent", "task"),                   profile = "profile", outcome = "chosen") get_matchups(tasks, A = list(party = "Democrat"),              B = list(party = "Republican"), outcome = "chosen") #> # A tibble: 582 × 3 #>    respondent  task A_wins #>         <int> <int>  <int> #>  1          3     4      0 #>  2          6     4      0 #>  3          8     1      0 #>  4          9     4      0 #>  5         10     2      0 #>  6         12     5      0 #>  7         14     1      0 #>  8         14     4      0 #>  9         16     2      1 #> 10         16     3      1 #> # ℹ 572 more rows

what: Is a matchup contrast well defined? — valid_contrast
title: Is a matchup contrast well defined? — valid_contrast
text: forced-choice contrast requires candidates B mutually exclusive: must pin least one shared attribute different levels. Otherwise single profile can satisfy sides, matchup includes tasks two candidates identical pinned attributes, outcome reflects display position rather real difference.

title: Is a matchup contrast well defined? — valid_contrast
code: valid_contrast(A, B)

title: Is a matchup contrast well defined? — valid_contrast
text: , B Named lists mapping attribute stems pinned levels.

title: Is a matchup contrast well defined? — valid_contrast
text: TRUE contrast well defined, FALSE otherwise.

title: Is a matchup contrast well defined? — valid_contrast
code: valid_contrast(list(party = "R"), list(party = "D"))   # TRUE #> [1] TRUE valid_contrast(list(party = "R"), list(gender = "F"))  # FALSE (no shared attr) #> [1] FALSE valid_contrast(list(party = "R"), list(party = "R"))   # FALSE (identical) #> [1] FALSE

title: conjointmatchups 0.0.0.9000
text: Initial scaffold. as_tasks() / as_profiles(): lossless pivot profile-long task-wide representations forced-choice conjoint. get_matchups(): extract subset tasks realizing specified contrast two profiles, clean binary outcome. valid_contrast() / contrast_message(): guard ill-defined (non-mutually-exclusive) contrasts. check_conjoint(): validate forced-choice data contract. afcp(): thin Average Feature Choice Probability estimator matchup.
